examples.title: Modular Factorial Calculation
examples.description: Demonstrates modular programming with separate functions for input, calculation, and display
examples.code:
MODULE Main
BEGIN
    DECLARE n, result
    CALL getNumber(n)
    CALL calculateFactorial(n, result)
    CALL displayResult(n, result)
END MODULE

MODULE getNumber(n)
BEGIN
    PRINT "Enter a non-negative integer: "
    INPUT n
END MODULE

MODULE calculateFactorial(n, result)
BEGIN
    DECLARE i
    result = 1
    FOR i = 1 TO n
        result = result * i
    END FOR
END MODULE

MODULE displayResult(n, result)
BEGIN
    PRINT n, "! = ", result
END MODULE
examples.explanation: This program demonstrates modular design with three separate functions: getNumber for input, calculateFactorial for processing, and displayResult for output. Each function has a single responsibility, achieving high cohesion and low coupling.

examples.title: Modular Program with Pass-by-Reference
examples.description: C program showing modular structure with pointers for pass-by-reference
examples.explanation: This program demonstrates modular programming with pass-by-reference using pointers. The findMinMax function returns two values (min and max) by using pointer parameters. Each function has a single, well-defined purpose, achieving high cohesion.

examples.title: Recursive Modular Factorial
examples.description: Python program demonstrating recursion with modular structure
examples.code:
# Module: Input Handler
def get_number():
    """Get non-negative integer from user"""
    n = int(input("Enter a non-negative integer: "))
    while n < 0:
        print("Please enter a non-negative number")
        n = int(input("Enter a non-negative integer: "))
    return n

# Module: Recursive Calculator
def factorial(n):
    """Calculate factorial recursively"""
    # Base case
    if n <= 1:
        return 1
    # Recursive case
    return n * factorial(n - 1)

# Module: Output Handler
def display_result(n, result):
    """Display the factorial result"""
    print(f"{n}! = {result}")

# Main module
def main():
    number = get_number()
    result = factorial(number)
    display_result(number, result)

# Entry point
if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
examples.explanation: This Python program demonstrates modular recursive programming. Each function (get_number, factorial, display_result) handles one aspect. The factorial function uses recursion with a base case (n <= 1) that terminates the recursion.

examples.title: Header and Source File Structure
examples.description: Demonstrates multi-file modular programming with header and source separation
examples.explanation: This demonstrates proper multi-file modular programming. calc.h contains function prototypes (interface), calc.c contains implementations, and main.c uses the module. The #ifndef/#endif guards prevent multiple inclusions. This structure allows separate compilation and code reuse.
